text: Background Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) are the most widely used non-viral vector for gene delivery. Physical characteristics such as size, surface charge and PEGylation of LNPs could play important roles in their in vivo and in vitro behavior. However, it is still unclear that which characteristic of LNPs could affect the transfection efficiency and distribution of LNPs after intramuscular administration, and how PEGylated lipids affect mRNA delivery. Methods Herein, a range of mRNA-LNPs formulations were prepared by adjusting the diameter, charge of lipid components and the types and content of PEGylated lipids. The effects of these variables on the transfection efficacy of the mRNA in vivo following intramuscular administration were assessed using a luciferase encoding mRNA, named as mLuc. The distribution of LNPs in vivo was also evaluated by ICP-MS, as well as the influence of PEGylated lipids on the internalization and escape behavior from the lysosome of mRNA-LNPs. Conclusion This study revealed that smaller diameter and neutral surface charge led to higher transfection efficiency of mRNA-LNPs following intramuscular administration. Moreover, varying PEGylated lipids with different acyl chains showed different cellular uptake behaviors and transfection efficacy in vivo. These results suggest that size, charge of LNPs and the structure of PEGylated lipids are important for intramuscular mRNA delivery. The findings of this study can significantly facilitate our understanding of the design of LNPs for mRNA therapy.
